en.extract:
The spotted pardalote (Pardalotus punctatus) is a small passerine bird native to eastern and southern Australia, at elevations of up to 2,000 metres (6,600 ft). It is part of the pardalote family, Pardalotidae. One of the smallest of all Australian birds at 8 to 10 centimetres (3.1 to 3.9 in) in length, and one of the most colourful; it is sometimes known as the diamondbird. Although moderately common in all of the reasonably fertile parts of Australia (the east coast, the south-east, and the south-west corner) it is seldom seen closely enough to enable identification. 
Three subspecies are recognised. The wet tropics spotted pardalote (subspecies militaris) is found in northeastern Queensland, while the distinctive subspecies, the yellow-rumped pardalote (subspecies xanthopyge), is mostly found in drier inland regions of southern Australia, particularly in semi-arid Mallee woodlands. Also occasionally found nesting in burrows in semi-rainforest areas inland from the coast in Mid North Coast NSW.


Taxonomy
The spotted pardalote was described by English naturalist George Shaw and drawn by Frederick Polydore Nodder in the 1792 work The Naturalist's Miscellany: Or, Coloured Figures Of Natural Objects; Drawn and Described Immediately From Nature. Calling it Pipra punctata, or speckled manakin, Shaw conceded that nothing had been reported of its habits in New Holland (Australia). Early settlers of New South Wales knew it as the Diamond Bird, on account of the spots on its plumage, and John Gould called it the spotted diamond-bird. Other early names include diamond sparrow, bank diamond and diamond dyke, the last two relating to its nest burrows in riverbanks. Indigenous people from lowlands and Perth districts of southern Western Australia knew it as widopwidop and bilyabit, though the terms were also used for the striated pardalote. Headache bird is a colloquial name given it because of the repetitive "sleep-may-be" call uttered in the breeding season.
The species was placed in the new genus Pardalotus by Louis Pierre Vieillot in 1816, who also coined the word "pardalote". Within the genus, its closest relative is the forty-spotted pardalote (Pardalotus quadragintus) based on size and plumage similarities.
Three subspecies are recognised. The nominate subspecies (P. punctatus punctatus) is found from southeastern Queensland through eastern New South Wales, eastern and southern Victoria and into southeastern South Australia, as well as southwestern Western Australia. It is also found across eastern and northwestern Tasmania.
The yellow-rumped pardalote (P. punctatus xanthopyge) was considered for many years to be a separate species native to dryer inland southern Australia. It was described in 1867 amid some controversy. Amateur ornithologist Edward Pierson Ramsay, then 24 years old, recorded that a specimen at the Australian Museum that had been collected by John Leadbeater near the Murray River differed in its plumage from the typical spotted pardalote. The director of the museum, Gerard Krefft, lent the specimen to Ramsay to describe, which he did as Pardalotus chrysoprymnus in a manuscript on 10 December 1866. Krefft advised him that Leadbeater was pushing for the species to be named after him, hence the paper was read but not published in London at a meeting of the Zoological Society of London on 28 February 1867. Meanwhile, Professor Frederick McCoy of the  National Museum of Natural History and Geology in Melbourne also published a description of the species from a specimen collected near Swan Hill, in The Australasian newspaper on 29 December 1866, which was formally described on 1 March 1867. McCoy named it Pardalotus xanthopygus, or yellow-backed diamondbird. Ramsay suspected that discussion of his description prompted McCoy to publish his own description; however, McCoy countered that they had been aware it was a separate species for some time. In any case, McCoy's description stood and Ramsay's was consigned to synonymy.
In a 1983 paper, Lester Short and colleagues noted the similarity of plumage and calls between the two taxa and occurrence of hybrid specimens from Victoria where the two forms overlapped. John Woinarski found that around Bendigo (where both taxa occur), more pairs appeared to contain members of both forms than not. Western Australian ornithologist Julian Ford felt evidence of hybridization in Western Australia was lacking and also wondered whether land clearing and habitat alteration had promoted hybridization in southeastern Australia. In their 1999 Directory of Australian Birds, Richard Schodde and Ian Mason relegated the yellow-rumped pardalote to subspecies status on account of the intermediate characteristics of subspecies militaris and the widespread hybridization in southeastern Australia. They felt Ford's evidence for lack of interbreeding in Western and South Australia was not strong, but conceded fieldwork in Western Australia was needed.
The Wet Tropics spotted pardalote (Pardalotus punctatus militaris) is found in coastal central-northern Queensland. It has features in common with both other subspecies.


Description

Weighing around 6 grams (0.21 oz), the spotted pardalote is 8 to 10 cm (3.1 to 3.9 in) long. The adult male of the nominate subspecies has grey-brown upperparts with numerous paler buff spots, a black crown, wings and tail all with white spots, white eyebrows and reddish rump. The underparts are pale-buff-cinnamon, darkening to a more ochre at the breast, with a demarcated yellow throat and vent. The female is duller overall. The yellow-rumped subspecies is larger overall with a relatively smaller bill. The adult male has finer, white spots on its back, a bright yellow rump, and a cream breast. The adult female has finer spots than the adult female of the nominate subspecies. The Wet Tropics subspecies is smaller with a relatively larger bill. The adult male has a reddish rump and pale- to cinnamon-buff underparts.


Distribution and habitat
George Caley reported that it was not common around Sydney even in early settlement days. Spotted pardalote numbers appear to be declining, especially in urban areas, but the species is not considered endangered at this time.


Nesting
Spotted pardalotes breed between August or September to December or January—generally earlier in the year in northern parts of their range and later in southern areas. The nest is an underground horizontal oval chamber lined with shredded bark, linked by a tunnel 0.5 to 1.5 metres (1 ft 8 in to 4 ft 11 in) long to a hole in the side of a riverbank or slope in a shaded location. The chamber is generally higher than the entrance tunnel, presumably to avoid flooding. Birds have used carpet rolls and garage roll-a-doors to nest in on occasion. Pairs breed once a year, producing a clutch of 3 to 4 round shiny white eggs 16 mm (0.63 in) long by 13 mm (0.51 in) wide. The eggs are incubated for 19 days until they hatch, with nestlings spending another 21 days in the nest.
Pairs make soft, whistling wheet-wheet calls to one another throughout the day, which carry for quite a distance. One of the difficulties in locating a pardalote is that the contact call is in fact two calls: an initial call and an almost instant response, and thus can come from two different directions.


Gallery


References


External links

Spotted Pardalote videos, photos & sounds on the Internet Bird Collection.

{{Short description|Species of bird}} {{Speciesbox | name = Spotted pardalote | image = Pardalotus punctatus 2 - Glen Davis.jpg | image_caption = Male, New South Wales | image2 = Pardalotus punctatus - Glen Davis.jpg | image2_caption = Female, New South Wales | status = LC | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = {{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Pardalotus punctatus'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22704490A93971454 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22704490A93971454.en |access-date=12 November 2021}} | genus = Pardalotus | species = punctatus | authority = ([[George Shaw (biologist)|Shaw]], 1792) | range_map = Spotted Pardalote Range.jpg | range_map_caption = Approximate distribution }} The '''spotted pardalote''' ('''''Pardalotus punctatus''''') is a small [[passerine]] bird native to eastern and southern Australia, at elevations of up to {{convert|2000|m|ft}}. It is part of the [[pardalote]] family, Pardalotidae. One of the smallest of all Australian birds at {{convert|8|to|10|cm}} in length, and one of the most colourful; it is sometimes known as the diamondbird. Although moderately common in all of the reasonably fertile parts of [[Australia]] (the east coast, the south-east, and the south-west corner) it is seldom seen closely enough to enable identification. {{Citation needed|date=June 2012}} Three subspecies are recognised. The '''wet tropics spotted pardalote''' (subspecies ''militaris'') is found in northeastern Queensland, while the distinctive subspecies, the '''yellow-rumped pardalote''' (subspecies ''xanthopyge''), is mostly found in drier inland regions of southern Australia, particularly in semi-arid [[Mallee (habit)|Mallee]] woodlands. Also occasionally found nesting in burrows in semi-rainforest areas inland from the coast in [[Mid North Coast]] NSW. ==Taxonomy== The spotted pardalote was described by English naturalist [[George Shaw (biologist)|George Shaw]] and drawn by [[Frederick Polydore Nodder]] in the 1792 work ''The Naturalist's Miscellany: Or, Coloured Figures Of Natural Objects; Drawn and Described Immediately From Nature''. Calling it ''Pipra punctata'', or speckled manakin, Shaw conceded that nothing had been reported of its habits in New Holland (Australia).{{cite book | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/44515430 | volume=4 | pages= 111–13 | title=The naturalist's miscellany, or Coloured figures of natural objects | author=Shaw, George | publisher=Nodder & Co. | location=London, United Kingdom | year=1792}} Early settlers of New South Wales knew it as the Diamond Bird, on account of the spots on its plumage,{{cite journal|last1=Vigors |first1=Nicholas Aylward|author2=Horsfield, Thomas|year=1827|title=A description of the Australian birds in the collection of the Linnean Society; with an attempt at arranging them according to their natural affinities |journal=Transactions of the Linnean Society of London |volume=15|pages= 170–331 [237–38]|doi= 10.1111/j.1095-8339.1826.tb00115.x|url=https://zenodo.org/record/1447478}} and John Gould called it the spotted diamond-bird.{{cite book|last=Gould|first=John|author-link=John Gould|title=Handbook to the Birds of Australia|volume=1 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IH9IAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA157 |year=1865 |publisher=self-published |isbn=1-116-37820-5|pages=157–58}} Other early names include diamond sparrow, bank diamond and diamond dyke, the last two relating to its nest burrows in riverbanks.{{cite book|last1=Gray|first1=Jeannie|last2=Fraser|first2=Ian|title=Australian Bird Names: A Complete Guide|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=z6ia8rqTa7EC&pg=PA155|year=2013|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|location=Collingwood, Victoria |isbn=978-0-643-10471-6|page=191}} Indigenous people from lowlands and Perth districts of southern Western Australia knew it as ''widopwidop'' and ''bilyabit'', though the terms were also used for the [[striated pardalote]].{{cite journal|author=Abbott, Ian | title=Aboriginal names of bird species in south-west Western Australia, with suggestions for their adoption into common usage |year= 2009 |journal= Conservation Science Western Australia Journal |volume= 7 |issue=2 |pages=213–78 [259] |url=http://www.dpaw.wa.gov.au/images/documents/about/science/cswa/articles/14.pdf}} ''Headache bird'' is a colloquial name given it because of the repetitive "sleep-may-be" call uttered in the breeding season.{{cite web | url=http://australianmuseum.net.au/spotted-pardalote | title=Spotted pardalote | work=Australian Museum website | author= Evans, Ondine | date= 25 September 2012 | access-date=12 September 2016 | publisher=Australian Museum }} The species was placed in the new genus ''Pardalotus'' by [[Louis Pierre Vieillot]] in 1816, who also coined the word "pardalote".{{cite book |last= Vieillot |first=Louis Pierre |title=Analyse d'une Nouvelle Ornithologie Élémentaire |year= 1816 |page=31 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12830273 |publisher=Déterville |location=Paris, France}} Within the genus, its closest relative is the [[forty-spotted pardalote]] (''Pardalotus quadragintus'') based on size and plumage similarities. Three subspecies are recognised. The nominate subspecies (''P. punctatus punctatus'') is found from southeastern Queensland through eastern New South Wales, eastern and southern Victoria and into southeastern South Australia, as well as southwestern Western Australia. It is also found across eastern and northwestern Tasmania. The yellow-rumped pardalote (''P. punctatus xanthopyge'') was considered for many years to be a separate species native to dryer inland southern Australia. It was described in 1867 amid some controversy. Amateur ornithologist [[Edward Pierson Ramsay]], then 24 years old, recorded that a specimen at the Australian Museum that had been collected by John Leadbeater near the Murray River differed in its plumage from the typical spotted pardalote. The director of the museum, [[Gerard Krefft]], lent the specimen to Ramsay to describe, which he did as ''Pardalotus chrysoprymnus'' in a manuscript on 10 December 1866. Krefft advised him that Leadbeater was pushing for the species to be named after him, hence the paper was read but not published in London at a meeting of the [[Zoological Society of London]] on 28 February 1867. Meanwhile, Professor [[Frederick McCoy]] of the [[Melbourne Museum|National Museum of Natural History and Geology]] in [[Melbourne]] also published a description of the species from a specimen collected near Swan Hill, in ''The Australasian'' newspaper on 29 December 1866, which was formally described on 1 March 1867. McCoy named it ''Pardalotus xanthopygus'', or yellow-backed diamondbird.{{cite journal|last=McCoy |first=Frederick |date=1867|title=XXXI.—On two new species of birds found in Victoria |journal=Annals and Magazine of Natural History|volume=19|issue=3|pages=184–85| url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/22193020|doi=10.1080/00222936708679752 }} Ramsay suspected that discussion of his description prompted McCoy to publish his own description; however, McCoy countered that they had been aware it was a separate species for some time. In any case, McCoy's description stood and Ramsay's was consigned to synonymy.{{cite journal|last=Hindwood |first=K.A.|date=1950|title=''Pardalotus xanthopygus'': A competition in 'Christening'.|journal=Emu|volume=49|issue=3|pages=205–08| doi=10.1071/MU949205 |bibcode=1950EmuAO..49..205H }} In a 1983 paper, Lester Short and colleagues noted the similarity of plumage and calls between the two taxa and occurrence of hybrid specimens from Victoria where the two forms overlapped.{{cite journal | author=Short, Lester L. | author2=Horne, Jennifer F.M. | author3=Schodde, Richard | title= Vocal behaviour, morphology, and hybridization of Australian Spotted and Yellow-rumped Pardalotes (Aves, ''Pardalotus'') | journal= American Museum Novitates | year= 1983 | issue=2756 |pages=1–15 |url=http://digitallibrary.amnh.org/bitstream/handle/2246/5311/N2756.pdf?sequence=1 }} John Woinarski found that around Bendigo (where both taxa occur), more pairs appeared to contain members of both forms than not.{{cite journal | title=Interbreeding of the Spotted and Yellow-rumped Pardalotes ''Pardalotus punctatus'' and ''P. xanthopygus'' | author=Woinarski, John C.Z. | journal= Emu | volume=84|issue=2| pages= 80–86 | year=1984 | doi=10.1071/MU9840080 | bibcode=1984EmuAO..84...80W }} Western Australian ornithologist [[Julian Ralph Ford|Julian Ford]] felt evidence of hybridization in Western Australia was lacking and also wondered whether land clearing and habitat alteration had promoted hybridization in southeastern Australia.{{cite journal | author=Ford, Julian | title= Hybrid zones in Australian birds| journal= Emu | year= 1987| volume=87|issue=3| pages= 158–78 | doi=10.1071/MU9870158 | bibcode= 1987EmuAO..87..158F| citeseerx= 10.1.1.694.3006}} In their 1999 ''Directory of Australian Birds'', Richard Schodde and Ian Mason relegated the yellow-rumped pardalote to subspecies status on account of the intermediate characteristics of subspecies ''militaris'' and the widespread hybridization in southeastern Australia. They felt Ford's evidence for lack of interbreeding in Western and South Australia was not strong, but conceded fieldwork in Western Australia was needed. The Wet Tropics spotted pardalote (''Pardalotus punctatus militaris'') is found in coastal central-northern Queensland. It has features in common with both other subspecies.{{Cite book | author = Schodde, Richard | author2 = Mason, Ian J. | author2-link = Ian J. Mason |year=1999|title=The Directory of Australian Birds: Passerines. A Taxonomic and Zoogeographic Atlas of the Biodiversity of Birds in Australia and its Territories | location= Collingwood, Victoria |publisher= CSIRO Publishing |pages=122–23|isbn=978-0-643-10293-4 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XXbJoaGF36YC&q=Pardalotus+punctatus&pg=PT1216}} ==Description== [[File:Spotted Pardalote (Pardalotus punctatus) male (21325231196).jpg|thumb|left|The reddish rump of the nominate subspecies is clearly visible (Strangways, Victoria).]] Weighing around {{convert|6|g}}, the spotted pardalote is {{convert|8|to|10|cm|abbr=on}} long.{{cite web|url= http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/species/Pardalotus-punctatus | title= Spotted pardalote | publisher=Birds in Backyards| access-date=13 September 2016}} The adult male of the nominate subspecies has grey-brown upperparts with numerous paler buff spots, a black crown, wings and tail all with white spots, white eyebrows and reddish rump. The underparts are pale-buff-cinnamon, darkening to a more ochre at the breast, with a demarcated yellow throat and vent. The female is duller overall.{{cite book |title= A Field Guide to Australian Birds. Volume Two: Passerines |author= Slater, Peter|year= 1974|publisher= Rigby |location= Adelaide |isbn= 0-85179-813-6 |page=209}} The yellow-rumped subspecies is larger overall with a relatively smaller bill. The adult male has finer, white spots on its back, a bright yellow rump, and a cream breast. The adult female has finer spots than the adult female of the nominate subspecies. The Wet Tropics subspecies is smaller with a relatively larger bill. The adult male has a reddish rump and pale- to cinnamon-buff underparts. ==Distribution and habitat== George Caley reported that it was not common around Sydney even in early settlement days. Spotted pardalote numbers appear to be declining, especially in urban areas,{{cite web| url= http://birdsinbackyards.net/feature/insectivores.cfm| title= Small insect-eating birds| publisher= Birds in Backyards| date= 9 November 2009| access-date= 9 August 2011| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20110812033126/http://birdsinbackyards.net/feature/insectivores.cfm| archive-date= 12 August 2011}} but the species is not considered endangered at this time. ==Nesting== Spotted pardalotes breed between August or September to December or January—generally earlier in the year in northern parts of their range and later in southern areas. The nest is an underground horizontal oval chamber lined with shredded bark, linked by a tunnel {{convert|0.5|to|1.5|m}} long to a hole in the side of a riverbank or slope in a shaded location. The chamber is generally higher than the entrance tunnel, presumably to avoid flooding. Birds have used carpet rolls and garage roll-a-doors to nest in on occasion. Pairs breed once a year, producing a clutch of 3 to 4 round shiny white eggs {{convert|16|mm|abbr=on}} long by {{convert|13|mm|abbr=on}} wide.{{cite book | last = Beruldsen | first = Gordon | title = Australian Birds: Their Nests and Eggs | publisher = self | year = 2003 | location = Kenmore Hills, Qld | isbn = 0-646-42798-9 | page = 288}} The eggs are incubated for 19 days until they hatch, with nestlings spending another 21 days in the nest. Pairs make soft, whistling ''wheet-wheet'' calls to one another throughout the day, which carry for quite a distance. One of the difficulties in locating a pardalote is that the contact call is in fact two calls: an initial call and an almost instant response, and thus can come from two different directions. ==Gallery== File:Spotted Pardalote postmans.ogg | {{center|[[Samsonvale, Queensland]], Australia }} File:Spotted Pardalote armstrongck.ogv | {{center|Singing male ([[Armstrong Creek, Queensland]], Australia) }} File:Spotted Pardalotes burrowing.ogg | {{center|Male and female burrowing
([[Thirlmere, New South Wales|Thirlmere NSW]], Australia) }} File:Pardalotus punctatus female with nesting material - Risdon Brook.jpg | {{center|Female with nesting material
([[Risdon Vale, Tasmania|Risdon Brook, Tasmania]], Australia) }} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Pardalotus punctatus}} * [http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/spotted-pardalote-pardalotus-punctatus Spotted Pardalote videos, photos & sounds] on the Internet Bird Collection. {{Taxonbar|from=Q573169}} [[Category:Articles containing video clips]] [[Category:Birds described in 1792|spotted pardalote]] [[Category:Birds of Victoria (state)]] [[Category:Endemic birds of Australia]] [[Category:Pardalote]] [[Category:Taxa named by George Shaw]]

zh.extract:
斑翅食蜜鸟（台湾作斑啄果鳥，学名：Pardalotus punctatus），是雀形目斑食蜜鸟科斑食蜜鸟属的一种鸟类。
斑翅食蜜鸟体重约8.7克，翼长约56.5毫米，嘴峰长约8毫米，喙宽度约3.2毫米，喙厚度约3.7毫米，跗蹠长约17.4毫米，尾长约27.6毫米。斑翅食蜜鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为杂食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
P. p. millitaris：分布于昆士兰州东北部。
P. p. punctatus：分布于昆士兰州东南部至南澳大利亚州、塔斯马尼亚州；南澳大利亚州西南部。
P. p. xanthopyge：分布于西澳大利亚州西南部至维多利亚州西北部和新南威尔士州南部。
